exercises.title: The One-Week Time Audit
exercises.description: Track every 30-minute block for one week. Use a simple spreadsheet with columns: Time, Activity, Category (Work/Personal/Wasted), Energy Level (1-5). At the end of the week, calculate totals for each category and identify your biggest time-wasters.
hints: Set phone alarms every 30 minutes to log your activity
Be honest — include social media, TV, and scrolling
Look for patterns in your energy levels throughout the day

solution.code:
Time Audit Results - Week 1:

Total hours: 168 (24h x 7 days)

Breakdown:
- Sleep: 52 hours (31%) - Good
- Deep work: 14 hours (8%) - Too low
- Administrative: 20 hours (12%) - High
- Meetings: 12 hours (7%) - Moderate
- Personal care: 15 hours (9%) - Good
- Leisure: 30 hours (18%) - Moderate
- Social media/scrolling: 15 hours (9%) - Too high
- Commute: 10 hours (6%) - Expected
- Wasted/unaccounted: 10 hours (6%) - Need to track better

Key findings:
1. Social media takes 15 hours/week — could reduce by 10 hours
2. Deep work is only 14 hours — should be 20-25 hours
3. Energy peaks at 9-11 AM and 2-4 PM
4. Lowest energy: 1-2 PM (post-lunch dip)

Action plan:
- Set 30-min social media limit daily (save 7 hours/week)
- Schedule deep work during 9-11 AM and 2-4 PM
- Take a 20-min walk during 1-2 PM instead of scrolling

exercises.title: The Weekly Planning Ritual
exercises.description: Every Sunday evening, spend 30 minutes planning your week. (1) Review last week's wins and lessons. (2) Identify your top 3 priorities for the week. (3) Time block your calendar with deep work, meetings, exercise, and personal time. (4) Identify potential obstacles and how you'll handle them.
hints: Start with your top 3 priorities and build around them
Schedule deep work first — everything else fits around it

solution.code:
Weekly Planning - Week of July 21:

Last week review:
- Win: Finished project proposal ahead of deadline
- Lesson: I'm most productive in the morning — protect those hours
- Improvement: Need to reduce meeting time

Top 3 priorities:
1. Complete Q3 budget analysis (due Friday)
2. Prepare presentation for leadership meeting (Wednesday)
3. Review team member's work and provide feedback (ongoing)

Time-blocked calendar:
- Monday: Deep work (9-12), Lunch (12-1), Meetings (1-3), Admin (3-5)
- Tuesday: Deep work (9-11), Meetings (11-12), Deep work (2-4), Admin (4-5)
- Wednesday: Presentation prep (9-11), Leadership meeting (2-3), Follow-up (3-5)
- Thursday: Deep work (9-12), Team feedback (2-3), Budget analysis (3-5)
- Friday: Budget analysis (9-12), Weekly review (3-4), Planning (4-5)

Potential obstacles:
- Unexpected meetings → Block 'focus time' on calendar as 'busy'
- Team questions → Set office hours: 10-11 AM and 3-4 PM only
- Low energy after lunch → Take a 20-min walk, then do lighter tasks

exercises.title: The Deep Work Challenge
exercises.description: For one week, schedule 2 hours of deep work each day. Rules: (1) No phone, email, or messaging apps. (2) Work on ONE important task. (3) Track your output. (4) Note how you feel before and after. Compare your output to a normal week.
hints: Start with 90 minutes if 2 hours feels too long
Use a timer — knowing there's an end point helps focus
Prepare everything you need before starting

solution.code:
Deep Work Challenge - Results:

Day 1: 90 minutes on report. Felt anxious at first (checking phone), but after 20 min, got into flow. Finished 60% of report.

Day 2: 2 hours on presentation. Used Pomodoro (25 min work, 5 min break). Finished first draft. Felt accomplished.

Day 3: 2 hours on budget analysis. Hardest day — couldn't focus. Switched to a walk after 45 min, came back and finished.

Day 4: 2 hours on proposal. Best day — flow state for 90 minutes. Finished proposal completely.

Day 5: 2 hours on team feedback. Productive but emotionally draining. Needed more breaks.

Day 6: 2 hours on research. Enjoyed it. Discovered insights I would have missed while multitasking.

Day 7: 2 hours on planning. Set up next week's deep work blocks.

Total deep work: 13 hours (vs. 8 hours the week before)
Output: Completed 3 major projects (vs. 1 the week before)
Quality: Received positive feedback on 2 deliverables

Key insight: Deep work produces 2-3x more output than distracted work, and it feels better too.
